ending: Matilda ends with the triumph of the gentle over the cruel. Having mastered her secret power of moving things with her mind, Matilda uses it to rescue her beloved teacher, Miss Honey, from the tyranny of the headmistress, Miss Trunchbull, who is also Miss Honey's aunt and the thief of her father's house and inheritance. In front of the class, Matilda makes a piece of chalk write on the blackboard as if guided by the ghost of Miss Honey's dead father, demanding that Trunchbull return what she stole and leave forever. Convinced she is being haunted, Trunchbull faints and then abandons the school for good, and no one hears from her again. Freed at last, Miss Honey reclaims her family home and money and becomes headmistress, while the school turns into a happy place. Meanwhile Matilda's father's crooked car business finally catches up with him, and the Wormwoods must flee to Spain in a hurry. Matilda begs to be allowed to remain with Miss Honey, and her indifferent parents agree without a fuss. The two are left to make a loving home together, and Matilda, now properly challenged in her schoolwork, finds her strange power quietly slips away, no longer needed.
in_short: Matilda Wormwood is a brilliant little girl, reading adult books and doing advanced sums, but her crude, dishonest father and vain mother ignore and belittle her, so she quietly punishes their cruelty with clever pranks. At school she meets a kind teacher, Miss Honey, who recognizes her genius, and a monstrous headmistress, Miss Trunchbull, who terrorizes the children. When Trunchbull wrongly blames her, Matilda discovers she can move objects with her mind. Miss Honey confides that Trunchbull is the aunt who raised her cruelly, seized her late father's house and money, and may have caused his death. Determined to help her friend, Matilda uses her secret power to make chalk write on the blackboard as though from the ghost of Miss Honey's father, ordering Trunchbull to give everything back and leave. The terrified headmistress flees for good; Miss Honey regains her home and inheritance. When Matilda's father must suddenly flee the country over his crimes, Matilda is allowed to stay behind and live happily with Miss Honey.

recaps.text: Matilda Wormwood is a tiny girl of remarkable intelligence, able to read and do arithmetic far beyond her years, but her crude, dishonest father, a crooked used-car dealer, and her vain, television-obsessed mother regard her as a nuisance and value only her older brother. Ignored at home, Matilda walks to the library on her own and works her way through grown-up novels, building a secret life of the mind. Hurt and angered by her parents' meanness and dishonesty, she begins to devise clever, harmless pranks to punish their bad behavior: she doctors her father's hair tonic, uses a hidden parrot to convince the family the house is haunted, and superglues his hat to his head. These small acts of justice give the neglected, gifted child a way to assert herself against the grown-ups who dismiss and mistreat her, establishing her as brave and resourceful well before she reaches school.

Matilda finally starts school and lands in the class of Miss Honey, a gentle young teacher who is astonished to discover just how brilliant her tiny pupil is, reading and calculating like an adult. Miss Honey tries to have Matilda moved up to a more challenging class, but she must contend with the school's monstrous headmistress, Miss Trunchbull, an enormous, terrifying woman who loathes children and rules Crunchem Hall through cruelty and fear. Trunchbull invents grotesque punishments and terrorizes pupils and staff alike, and she flatly refuses to believe in Matilda's gifts. Miss Honey, kind but timid and oddly impoverished, takes a special interest in the child and even visits her careless parents to praise her, only to be rudely rebuffed. Matilda, meanwhile, begins to find her feet among her new classmates and comes face to face with the headmistress's brutality, sharpening her sense that the cruel and dishonest adults around her deserve to be resisted.

Life at Crunchem Hall under Miss Trunchbull grows steadily more frightening. The children witness her savage punishments, including forcing a boy, Bruce Bogtrotter, to eat a gigantic chocolate cake before the whole school, and they live in dread of the Chokey, a cramped cupboard lined with spikes. Lavender plays a daring trick by slipping a newt into the headmistress's water jug during a class inspection. When Trunchbull unleashes her fury and wrongly blames Matilda, the child, gripped by a surge of concentrated anger, discovers that she can move objects with the power of her mind alone: staring hard at the jug, she tips it over onto the headmistress. Astonished and thrilled by this strange new ability, Matilda realizes she possesses a secret power, and she resolves to practice and understand it. Her extraordinary intellect has found an equally extraordinary companion in this hidden gift, giving her a possible means to confront a bully far too powerful to face head-on.

Matilda confides her secret power to Miss Honey and demonstrates it, tipping over a glass of water by will alone. Drawn closer together, the teacher invites Matilda to her tiny, almost bare cottage, where Matilda is shocked by how poor Miss Honey is. Miss Honey then shares her painful history: orphaned as a child, she was raised by a harsh, domineering aunt who bullied her and took control of the house and money her father had left her. That aunt, she reveals, is none other than Miss Trunchbull, and Miss Honey suspects the woman may even have been responsible for her father's supposedly accidental death, having seized his home and inheritance and kept Miss Honey poor and cowed ever since. Understanding now why the gentle teacher lives in fear and want, and burning with a sense of injustice, Matilda determines to use her secret power to help the person who has been kindest to her.

Matilda secretly practices her power until she can control it, then puts a bold plan into action in front of the whole class. Using her mind, she lifts a piece of chalk and makes it write on the blackboard by itself, pretending to be the ghost of Miss Honey's dead father, Magnus, and commanding Miss Trunchbull to give back the house and money she stole and to leave. Terrified by what she believes is a vengeful spirit, the headmistress collapses and then flees, never to return. With Trunchbull gone, Miss Honey recovers her family home and inheritance and returns to a happy life, and the school becomes a far kinder place. Around the same time, Matilda's father's shady car dealings catch up with him, and the family must suddenly flee the country to Spain. In the joyful final turn, Matilda asks to stay behind with Miss Honey, her parents carelessly agree, and the two, teacher and gifted child, are left to live happily together at last. Matilda's mind-moving power fades once she is placed in a more challenging class, but she no longer needs it.
